content: 2026-06 just in case you’re not aware, i maintain my own browser app for mastodon api compatible instance software. you can check it out at https://outpost-fe.w.on-t.work if you want, but i should warn you that it’s very much not finished, and given how eager the web development community as a whole seems to be embracing venture capital and language models (among other reasons like horrendous executive dysfunction) i admit i’m not very motivated to actually work on it. despite all that, i still use it daily for pretty much anything other than sharing files (as it does not support that yet), and given it’s relative obscurity i think there’s a few insights worth sharing in there. readable css classes if you open dev tools and check out the DOM, you may notice something a bit out of the ordinary compared to most “modern” single-page applications: the class names are surprisingly readable this was an intentional decision on my part, and the way i accomplished it does result in some developer experience concessions that may cause trouble at large, unorganized, likely enterprise, codebases, which is probably why they aren’t the defaults. for example, the Capitalized class names are from svelte’s component css scoping system, with the naming convention adjusted via cssHash. one thing to note here is that sveltekit does not apply cssHash on dev mode, and throws a pretty ugly warning telling you it’ll be disregarded. despite the warning, the production build does indeed respect the setting. one downside here is that, because i got rid of the hash, i can not call multiple components the same name. this has not once been a problem in my case. the _underscored class names are scoped to the Capitalized names, following the convention from various programming languages to prefix private member variables in a class with an underscore. i use the ._underscored selector during development, and in production, svelte’s preprocessor will automatically convert it to the .Capitalized._underscored selector required for proper scoping. the lowercase-dashed class names are css modules, with the naming convention adjusted via generateScopedName on vite’s end. one thing you may notice is that it’s pointed at the same function that generates the class names for svelte, because i believe in bundle sizes and a neat DOM, i use css modules for “components” whose only purpose is to adjust css, such as panels or icons (and before you say anything, i have built a code generator that makes icons a lot less of an hassle, and tree-shakable. i just have not backported it to this codebase yet) and finally, the Capitalized-dashed class names are a custom preprocessor, adjusted from an example by someone on github, in order to solve one my main pain-points around svelte, being the lack of any way to style child components. you can see that in action in the Timeline-item class in the example image, which is a Culled component that needs special styling to fit inside it’s Timeline parent. additionally, because the class names it generates are accessible in javascript, this has been quite useful for marking up components for javascript interactivity, which is demonstrated by Status-clickable above, which contains no styling and only serves to be a marker to stop propagation for Status’s onClick handler. global globals if you have not caught onto the flavor of autism motivating the design choices behind all this, you can also switch to the console tab, write _, and see what toys autocomplete brings up to you. _settings.display_accent is a visually striking one to play around with, but _api, _queryClient, or _bus feel a lot more interesting to expose to userscripts and such. ok that’s enough of the technical bits, let’s look at the design some. mobile -> ?? -> desktop the app is “responsive”, which just means i’ve managed to squeeze it to a mobile aspect ratio without losing too much. i personally don’t really care about “mobile-first” design, as the limited screen size can be motivation to throw out a lot of more interesting functionality. and also because this is really mainly for me, and i mainly use it on desktop. however, if you open the app and resize the window a little, you may notice something odd: the sidebar buttons go off-screen way before the window is actually narrow enough to deserve a mobile treatment. or at least, they would. what actually happens is that the sidebar switches to this, what i expect to be relatively rarely seen, icon-only mode: i determined the breakpoint in which this switch happens based on when the text of the buttons start going off-screen. the icons go off a bit before that, but i don’t see that as a problem, because you can still read the text. i actually have a bit of a soft spot for how this icon-only mode looks, but given the free space on wider screens, it just feels irresponsible to not place the text on there as well. and yes, the hover popup also shows up when you’re tabbing through the sidebar. although tab order is a bit wonky because mobile navigation on desktop, the “create post” button is at the bottom, to match other social media uis such as bluesky or misskey. on mobile, it moves to the center of the bottom bar, in order to highlight it’s place as the “ ”. because i do this with css instead of javascript, this does break the tabbing order slightly. i wish there was a better way to do this without js, but given the app does not work without it i may Just end up switching over if this becomes an issue. ok, let’s move on to stuff that you might have actually noticed threading through grouping mastodon, western misskey forks such as sharkey and iceshrimp, bluesky, all of these represent a thread of posts in a timeline by squeezing posts into one side, moving the avatar outside, and drawing a line between avatars. in more un-polished variants, the bottom-most post does not get this treatment, so it does not align with its ancestors. in my application, all posts are disconnected, in their own bubble, and only connect when they’re threaded together. additionally, to help lessen the visual noise of “🧵 1/x”, when someone’s replying to themselves, the author info and/or interaction buttons gets chopped off. the last part makes interacting with the parent of a self-reply slightly difficult without clicking to open it in full, and the timestamp gets hidden as well. however, given most uses of self-replies happen roughly around the same time, other apps also group posts together (otherwise the line they draw between avatars would look odd), but their separators are not as pronounced as mine, hence they need more emphasis than the (lack of) separators, compared to my approach. no display names? the keen eyed among you may have noticed that my app does not display “display names” by default, only showing handles until you click. every time i tried to fit display names on, posts looked too unbalanced. too “top heavy” i guess. i’m not sure if i can explain why, it’s just a vibe. by making the byline only a single line tall though, i did get some interesting design opportunities. replies and boosts, for example, show their author in pretty much the exact same way the post author is shown, instead of being relegated to de-emphasised plaintext. additionally, because they take so little space (except for the corner case where handles are too long, in which case we truncate with ellipsis), we can fit two next to each other, which is useful to represent “X replied to Y, and the reply got boosted by Z” and finally, just to make thing a bit less cluttered, self-replies and self-boosts (and self-boosts of self-replies) do not display the second byline altogether. also, you may have noticed that the reply icons are different between self-replies and replies to other people. as in the second case, the icon points from the avatar of the replier, to the avatar of the repliee (?), which is not a thing for the self-reply case. this does have the unfortunate side effect of self-boosts not displaying the boosting time and visibility, instead showing the values off the original post. i personally don’t see that as a problem, but i can understand why it may be one. currently, the app can only competently render image, video, and has basic but limited support for audio. one thing to quickly note, is that videos and audio have a minimum width, when images do not. this is simply to let firefox’s media controls show the volume slider, as without it you only get mute or full volume. this is stupid. when a post has more than one attachment, it gets rendered as a carousel. the scrollbar is intentionally made visible to let people infer how many items there are, and the first attachment has a max-width just long enough to get a peek of the second item in the carousel, so people know there’s more than one attachment. additionally, unlike very little other elements in the post body, this carousel runs to the edges of the post “box” instead of having the same margins the rest of the items do. this means attachments that scroll off disappear more naturally at the boundary of the post content, instead of “in mid air”. the current implementation of this “breakout” is a bit hacky, i’ve since then found a better approach involving css grid, but have not backported it yet. now here’s a bit of a rant: SIZING THESE IS A FUCKING NIGHTMARE. i want to support backends which do not provide dimensions for attachments, and i want the media to be as large as they are, within the bounds i’ve given them. and the only way i’ve managed to implement this is a horrendous hack, which itself resulted in clicking images opens up a lightbox with the alt text as a caption. this lightbox also contains a bad hack. on mobile or trackpads, it’ll scroll like butter because it’s pretty much pure css. on a mouse that only does vertical scrolling though, you have to treat it very carefully, and scroll just one wheel “tick” at a time. scroll like normal, and it’ll stutter and may not even go to the next item. this is because i have to use javascript in order to redirect vertical scrolling to horizontal, and i’m not aware of a solution that prevents this stutter without dropping inputs. i wish css had a solution for this. i could maybe hack together a rotation to make the lightbox logically be vertical, but look horizontal, but honestly, is this that niche of a use case? or am i just missing something. i feel liike i’m missing something. also, again, like the attachments under the posts, the native scroll bar is visible and acts as a replacement for the small dots you’d otherwise get in carousels like this. also, i have code that lets this happen: it only works for software federating ,
